title: IRGC Issues Urgent Evacuation Warning for Gulf Energy Hubs
summary: Iran's Revolutionary Guard has threatened to strike five major oil and gas facilities across Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Qatar, ordering immediate evacuations as tensions escalate following strikes on Iran's South Pars gasfield.
content:
Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps has issued a direct threat to strike critical oil and gas infrastructure across the Gulf, declaring five major energy facilities in Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Qatar as legitimate targets for imminent retaliation. The warning, broadcast via state media on Wednesday, orders all employees and residents to immediately evacuate the vicinity of the identified sites.

The IRGC has named the following facilities as direct targets for strikes expected in the coming hours: in Saudi Arabia, the SAMREF oil refinery and the Jubail petrochemical complex; in the United Arab Emirates, the Al Hosn gasfield; and in Qatar, the Ras Laffan processing facilities and the Mesaieed petrochemical complex.

The threat follows a devastating morning airstrike on Iran's South Pars gasfield — the world's largest natural gas reserve — which crippled several processing phases and ignited massive fires in Bushehr province. The IRGC stated that these energy centres are now viewed as extensions of US and Israeli military interests. An IRGC naval wing spokesperson declared that the era of strategic patience is over, warning that if Iranian energy infrastructure is destroyed, the region's energy will follow.

Panic has begun spreading across the Gulf's industrial zones. Reports indicate Qatar has commenced an emergency evacuation of Ras Laffan, while Saudi air defences are on high alert across the Eastern Province. Qatar's Foreign Ministry condemned the initial strike on South Pars as dangerous and irresponsible, warning that the shared nature of the field makes any escalation a direct threat to global energy security.

title: The Hollowed Center: Why Larijani's Death Changes Everything for Iran
summary: The confirmation of Ali Larijani's death on March 17, following a targeted Israeli air strike at his daughter's home in Pardis, isn't just another casualty—it's the removal of the Islamic Republic's 'brain trust' and leaves Iran without its most strategic player.
content:
In the high-stakes conflict currently unfolding, Iran just lost its most strategic player. The confirmation of Ali Larijani's death on March 17, following a targeted Israeli air strike at his daughter's home in Pardis, isn't just another casualty—it's the removal of the Islamic Republic's 'brain trust.'

Unlike the generals who have been targeted in recent weeks, Larijani wasn't a man of the barracks; he was a man of the boardroom. As the Secretary of the Supreme National Security Council, he was the rare bridge between rigid ideology and cold, hard pragmatism. Following the assassination of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei on February 28, Larijani had emerged as the de facto strategist, the one man capable of managing the 'triple threat' currently suffocating Tehran: a regional war, a domestic uprising met with brutal crackdowns, and a nuclear program under literal fire.

Why his absence creates a vacuum:

The Loss of the 'Operator': Larijani was a survivor who had run state broadcasting, served as Speaker of Parliament for over a decade, and acted as a chief nuclear negotiator. He knew how the gears of the state turned in a way few others did.

A Fragile Succession: His death leaves the new Supreme Leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, without one of his father's most trusted and experienced advisors at a time when the leadership is increasingly forced into the shadows for security.

The Shift to the Military: With the 'pragmatic' civilian-political wing losing its heavyweights, power is drifting toward a fractured military leadership. As President Masoud Pezeshkian recently hinted, armed forces units may now be operating with broader autonomy. In practice, this means decisions may be taken more quickly, but with far less central coordination.

We aren't just looking at a country at war; we're looking at a state struggling to maintain its own architecture. With Larijani gone, the 'calculated strategy' he championed is likely to be replaced by a more volatile, reactionary posture—both in the regional conflict and in the repression of dissent at home.

title: At Least 100 Killed in Pakistani Air Strike on Kabul Drug Rehab Centre
summary: More than 100 people have been killed after a Pakistani air strike leveled a major drug treatment facility in the Afghan capital, according to forensic sources.
content:
KABUL — More than 100 people have been killed after a Pakistani air strike leveled a major drug treatment facility in the Afghan capital, according to forensic sources.

The strike targeted the Omid Addiction Treatment Hospital, a sprawling complex that was once a US military base. While forensic experts in Kabul say many victims are charred beyond recognition, Taliban officials claim the death toll could be as high as 400.

'Military Installations' vs. Civilian Care

Pakistan's Ministry of Information has denied intentionally striking the hospital, maintaining that its forces precisely targeted military installations and terrorist support infrastructure. However, Afghan health ministry spokesman Sharafat Zaman Amarkhail told the BBC there were no military targets in the vicinity of the rehab center.

International Outcry

The UN Assistance Mission in Afghanistan has condemned the attack and called for an immediate ceasefire. China has also stepped in to mediate, with Foreign Minister Wang Yi reportedly holding emergency calls with both counterparts in Kabul and Islamabad.

title: Israel Claims Assassination of Iran's Security Chief Larijani and Basij Commander
summary: In a major escalation on Day 18 of the conflict, Israel claims to have killed Ali Larijani and Gholamreza Soleimani. Tehran has not confirmed, and a handwritten note from Larijani has raised questions about proof of life.
content:
In a major escalation on Day 18 of the US-Israel war on Iran, Israel has claimed the assassination of two of Iran's most influential figures: Ali Larijani, Secretary of the Supreme National Security Council, and Gholamreza Soleimani, the commander of the internal Basij militia.

While Israeli Defense Minister Yoav Katz hailed the operations as a termination of the regime's leadership capabilities, Tehran has yet to officially confirm the deaths.

The 'Proof of Life' Mystery

Shortly after the Israeli announcement, Iranian state media circulated a handwritten note purportedly from Larijani. The note honors 84 sailors killed in a recent US attack, but analysts remain skeptical, noting it is unclear if the message was pre-recorded or truly a proof of life.

What This Means for the War

Despite the symbolic weight of these claims, experts warn against assuming a total collapse of the Iranian hierarchy. Analysts suggest that while these strikes are psychologically damaging, the Iranian system is designed for continuity, with deputies ready to step into vacated roles immediately.

title: Trump's confidence is undimmed — but every Iran option comes with risk
summary: The president is acting like business as usual, but rising gas prices and reluctant allies are making the Iran conflict a political headache that won't go away.
content:
For Donald Trump, the third week of the conflict with Iran looks a lot like any other week. On Monday, he held court at the White House for over an hour, touching on everything from the war effort to Kennedy Center renovations. Over the weekend, he was on the golf course in Florida.

On the surface, the man is unbothered. But beneath that familiar exterior, a more complicated truth is brewing — every American president eventually learns that you might be able to ignore a war, but a war will not ignore you.

The ally problem

Trump's weekend call for a global coalition to secure the Strait of Hormuz has been met with a collective shrug from key allies. The UK, Japan, Australia and several European powers have politely declined to join a new military coalition. UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer made it clear that Britain would not be drawn into a wider war.

The political math: Gas prices

The average cost for a gallon of gas has shot up to $3.72 — a massive jump from $2.94 just a month ago. For a president who ran on fixing the economy, a gas price spike is the quickest way to lose the middle ground politically.

What happens now?

Trump can escalate and risk American lives and a political backlash. He can hold steady and hope allies come around. Or he can declare victory and go home, risking that Iran remains a threat. As every president before him has learned, wars have a habit of writing their own endings.
